subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15! In this unit you will learn all about angles and movement – the maths of turning, facing, and finding your way. Every time you turn a corner on the way to the masjid, point towards the direction of the sunrise, or tell your friend how to reach the market, you are already using angles and directions. This unit will help you describe those turns and directions with clear, correct mathematics.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain that an angle is an amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point.
Identify and make a right angle (a quarter turn, or 90°).
Compare angles to a right angle: greater than, less than, or equal to it.
Name the main types of angle: acute, right, obtuse and straight.
Use the four main compass points (North, East, South, West) and the four between them.
Describe turns as quarter, half, three-quarter and full turns.
Say whether a turn is clockwise or anticlockwise.
Follow and give directions for a route on a grid or simple map.

concepts.title: What Is an Angle?
concepts.explanation: An angle is the amount of turn between two lines that meet at a point. Say that carefully to yourself: an angle is an amount of turn. It is not a shape, and it is not about how long the lines are. It is only about how far around you turn. Picture Hassan standing in the school yard. He points one arm straight ahead at the gate. Now he keeps his feet still and swings his arm around to point at the well. The amount his arm swept around is the angle. If he swings only a little, the angle is small. If he swings far…
concepts.example: Every angle has these parts. The vertex is the point where the two lines meet. The arms are the two straight lines going out from the vertex. When you look at the corner of a page in your book, the very corner point is…

concepts.title: The Right Angle
concepts.explanation: The most important angle to know is the right angle. A right angle is a quarter turn – one quarter of the way around a full circle. Its size is 90°. A right angle makes a neat, square corner, shaped like the letter L. Right angles are all around you. The corner of your book is a right angle. The corner of a window is a right angle. The corner of a door, a table, a tile on the floor, and a prayer mat – all right angles. A square and a rectangle each have four right angles, one at every corner. Once you start…
concepts.example: You can make your very own right angle measure out of a piece of paper, and it works beautifully. Take any piece of paper, even one with rough, torn edges. Fold it in half once. Then fold it in half again so the first…

concepts.title: Comparing Angles to a Right Angle
concepts.explanation: Once you know the right angle well, you can use it to describe every other angle. We compare an angle to a right angle and ask a simple question: is it greater than, less than, or equal to a right angle? This gives us three families of angle. A right angle is exactly equal to a right angle – it is exactly 90°, the square corner we already met.
concepts.example: An obtuse angle is greater than a right angle but still less than a straight line. It is bigger than 90° but smaller than 180°. Obtuse angles look wide and open, like a door opened far back or a book laid almost flat.…

concepts.title: The Compass Points
concepts.explanation: A compass is a tool that helps us find our way. It shows us the main directions, called the compass points. The four main compass points are North, East, South and West. We use the short letters N, E, S and W. We can be more exact by adding four more directions between the main points. Between North and East is Northeast (NE). Between South and East is Southeast (SE). Between South and West is Southwest (SW). Between North and West is Northwest (NW). Now we have eight compass points, spaced equally around the…
concepts.example: The compass points connect directly to right angles and turns. Starting at North and turning clockwise, one right angle (a quarter turn) takes you to East. Another right angle takes you to South. Another takes you to…

concepts.title: Turns – Quarter, Half, Three-Quarter and Full
concepts.explanation: We describe how far something turns using four special turns. Each one is a number of right angles, so each one is easy to picture. A quarter turn is one right angle. It is 90°. Example: turning from North to East.
concepts.example: A half turn is two right angles. It is 180°. Example: turning from North to South, so you end up facing the opposite way.

concepts.title: Clockwise and Anticlockwise
concepts.explanation: When something turns, it can turn in one of two directions. We use the words clockwise and anticlockwise to say which way. Clockwise means turning the same way as the hands of a clock move. If you look at a clock on the wall, the hands go from the top, round to the right, then down, then to the left, and back to the top. Going through the compass points, clockwise is the order North, East, South, West and back to North.
concepts.example: Anticlockwise (sometimes called counterclockwise) means turning the opposite way to a clock's hands. Going through the compass points, anticlockwise is the order North, West, South, East and back to North. Anticlockwise…

methods.title: How to name an angle
methods.example: An angle is smaller than a right angle. What type of angle is it?
steps: Compare the angle to a right angle (use your paper measure).
If it is smaller than a right angle, it is acute.
If it fits exactly, it is a right angle (90°).
If it is wider but not a straight line, it is obtuse.
If its arms make a straight line, it is a straight angle (180°).

methods.title: How to work out a turn
methods.example: Is an angle of 120° greater than, less than, or equal to a right angle?
steps: Note which direction you start facing.
Note how big the turn is (quarter, half, three-quarter, full).
Note which way you turn (clockwise or anticlockwise).
Move one right angle at a time around the compass to find where you finish.

methods.title: How to follow a route on a grid
methods.example: Zahra is facing North. She makes a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction does she face now?
steps: Start at the given square, facing the given direction.
Do each instruction one at a time, in order.
After each move, say which way you now face and where you are.
Stop when you reach the finish and check it makes sense.

methods.title: A half turn
methods.example: Idris is facing East. He makes a half turn. Which direction does he face now?
steps: Solution: A half turn (180°) always leaves you facing the opposite direction.
The opposite of East is West.
So Idris faces West.
(It does not matter whether he turns clockwise or anticlockwise for a half turn – he ends up in the same place.)

methods.title: Direction matters
methods.example: Omar faces North and makes a quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction does he face?
steps: Solution: Anticlockwise from North goes to West (the opposite way to clockwise).
So Omar faces West.
Compare this with Worked Example 3: the same size of turn, but the other direction, gives the opposite answer.

methods.title: Two turns in a row
methods.example: Maryam faces North. She makes a half turn, then a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction does she face at the end?
steps: Solution: Start North.
A half turn takes her to South.
A quarter turn clockwise from South takes her to West.
So Maryam finishes facing West.

workedExamples.title: Name the angle
workedExamples.prompt: An angle is smaller than a right angle. What type of angle is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: An angle smaller than a right angle (less than 90°) is an acute angle. Remember, acute angles are the small, sharp ones.

workedExamples.title: Compare to a right angle
workedExamples.prompt: Is an angle of 120° greater than, less than, or equal to a right angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A right angle is 90°. Since 120° is more than 90°, the angle is greater than a right angle. Because it is more than 90° but less than 180°, it is an obtuse angle.

workedExamples.title: A quarter turn
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra is facing North. She makes a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction does she face now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Clockwise from North goes to East. So after a quarter turn clockwise, Zahra faces East.

workedExamples.title: A half turn
workedExamples.prompt: Idris is facing East. He makes a half turn. Which direction does he face now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A half turn (180°) always leaves you facing the opposite direction. The opposite of East is West. So Idris faces West. (It does not matter whether he turns clockwise or anticlockwise for a half turn – he ends up in the same place.)

workedExamples.title: Direction matters
workedExamples.prompt: Omar faces North and makes a quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction does he face?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Anticlockwise from North goes to West (the opposite way to clockwise). So Omar faces West. Compare this with Worked Example 3: the same size of turn, but the other direction, gives the opposite answer.

workedExamples.title: Two turns in a row
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam faces North. She makes a half turn, then a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction does she face at the end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start North. A half turn takes her to South. A quarter turn clockwise from South takes her to West. So Maryam finishes facing West. Break the problem into one turn at a time and it becomes easy. Worked Example 7: How many right angles? A full turn is made of how many right angles, and how many degrees is that? Solution: A full turn is four right angles. Each right angle is 90°, so 4 × 90° = 360°. A full turn is 360°.

workedExamples.title: How many right angles?
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam faces North. She makes a half turn, then a quarter turn clockwise. Which direction does she face at the end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start North. A half turn takes her to South. A quarter turn clockwise from South takes her to West. So Maryam finishes facing West. Break the problem into one turn at a time and it becomes easy. Worked Example 7: How many right angles? A full turn is made of how many right angles, and how many degrees is that? Solution: A full turn is four right angles. Each right angle is 90°, so 4 × 90° = 360°. A full turn is 360°.

workedExamples.title: Follow a route
workedExamples.prompt: Hani starts at square (0, 0) facing North. She walks forward 3 squares, then makes a quarter turn clockwise and walks forward 4 squares. Which direction does she now face, and how far East has she moved?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: She walks 3 squares North first. Then a quarter turn clockwise from North makes her face East. She walks 4 squares East. So she now faces East and has moved 4 squares East (and 3 squares North from her start).

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: You face West and make a quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction do you face?
workedExamples.solution: South (anticlockwise from West goes to South).

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Fill the compass: between North and East is ___; between South and West is ___.

workedExamples.title: Which is the bigger turn each time
workedExamples.prompt: Which is the bigger turn each time: a) quarter turn or half turn b) three-quarter turn or full turn?
workedExamples.solution: a) half turn, b) full turn.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: You face East and make a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction do you face at the end?
workedExamples.solution: 1) East, three-quarter turn anticlockwise: East → North → West → South, so South.

activities.title: Fold Your Own Right-Angle Measure
steps: Take a piece of paper – even one with rough, torn edges works.
Fold it in half once, pressing the fold flat.
Fold it in half again so the first fold lies neatly on top of itself.
Open it slightly. The corner where the folds meet is a perfect right angle (90°).
Hold your right-angle corner against corners around the room to test which are true right angles.

activities.title: Right-Angle Hunt
activities.materials: your paper right-angle measure and a pencil.
steps: Walk around your home or classroom with your right-angle measure.
Find four true right angles (they fit your measure exactly). Draw them.
Find four angles that are greater than a right angle (obtuse). Draw them.
Find four angles that are less than a right angle (acute). Draw them.
Show a family member your collection and explain how you sorted each one.

activities.title: Compass Rose on the Floor
activities.materials: chalk, tape, or four labelled cards (N, E, S, W).
steps: Mark North, East, South and West on the floor, a right angle apart, like a big plus sign.
Stand in the middle facing North.
Call out a turn, such as ‘quarter turn clockwise’, and make it. Say which way you now face.
Try half turns and three-quarter turns. Check you land on the right direction each time.
Ask a partner to call the turns while you move, then swap over.

activities.title: Turn Game (Simon Says, Angles!)
activities.materials: yourself and one or more players.
steps: One player is the caller. They call turns like ‘Make a quarter turn anticlockwise!’
Everyone else makes the turn with their body.
The caller checks that everyone is facing the correct way.
Add compass points: ‘Face North!’ then ‘Now half turn – which way are you facing?’
Take turns being the caller. See who can follow the fastest without a mistake.

activities.title: Treasure Route Investigation
activities.materials: squared paper, a pencil, and a small object for ‘treasure’.
steps: Draw a grid of squares on your paper. Mark a START square and hide the ‘treasure’ on another square.
Write clear directions from START to the treasure using directions, turns and numbers of squares.
Give your directions to a partner and see if they reach the treasure.
Now find a different route to the same treasure and write those directions too.
How many different routes can you find? Record them and compare with a friend.

activities.title: Clock-Hand Angles (Family Task)
activities.materials: a clock with hands, or two strips of card pinned at one end to make ‘hands’.
steps: Set the hands to 3 o'clock. What type of angle do the hands make? (A right angle!)
Set them to 6 o'clock. What angle now? (A straight angle, 180°.)
Make an acute angle with the hands, then an obtuse angle.
Ask a family member to set an angle and challenge you to name it.
Talk about how the hands always turn clockwise – that is where the word comes from!

rules.title: Angle Rule
rules.text: An angle is the amount of turn between two lines that meet at a point. The length of the arms does not change the angle. Only the turn matters. We measure angles in degrees. A full turn is 360 degrees.

rules.title: Right-Angle Rule
rules.text: A right angle is a quarter turn. It is 90 degrees. It looks like a square corner (L). Two right angles make a straight line, 180 degrees (a half turn). Four right angles make a full turn, 360 degrees.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Comparing Angles Acute angle: smaller than a right angle (less than 90 degrees). Right angle: exactly 90 degrees. Obtuse angle: bigger than a right angle but less than a straight line (90 to 180 degrees). Straight angle: a half turn, exactly 180 degrees.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Turns and Directions Clockwise follows a clock: North to East to South to West. Anticlockwise is the opposite: North to West to South to East. Quarter turn = 90. Half turn = 180. Three-quarter turn = 270. Full turn = 360.

terms: The amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point
The point where the two lines of an angle meet
A quarter turn; a square corner; 90°
Smaller than a right angle (less than 90°)
Bigger than a right angle but less than 180°
North, East, South, West (and NE, SE, SW, NW)
Same way as a clock / the opposite way
commonMistakes: Longer arms mean a bigger angle
The angle is only the amount of turn
Acute is small and sharp; obtuse is wide and open
North, East, South, West never change – use a compass rose

realProblems.prompt: Liban starts at the well facing North. He walks 4 squares North, turns a quarter turn clockwise, and walks 3 squares. Which direction does he face at the end, and how far East has he travelled?
realProblems.answer: 1) He faces East and has travelled 3 squares East (and 4 North).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) He faces East and has travelled 3 squares East (and 4 North).

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Zahra spins around once completely to show off her new dress. Through how many degrees did she turn?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A robot at Nuur Academy is told: face North, turn a half turn, then a quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction does it end up facing?
realProblems.answer: North → half turn → South → quarter turn anticlockwise → East, so East.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: North → half turn → South → quarter turn anticlockwise → East, so East.

realProblems.prompt: Omar's prayer mat has four corners. What type of angle is each corner, and how many degrees do all four corners add up to?
realProblems.answer: Each corner is a right angle (90°); all four add up to 4 × 90° = 360°.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Each corner is a right angle (90°); all four add up to 4 × 90° = 360°.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha gives her brother directions to the sambusa stall: ‘Face East, walk 5 steps, turn a quarter turn clockwise, walk 2 steps.’ In which two compass directions did he walk?
realProblems.answer: He walked East, then after a quarter turn clockwise from East he faced South, so East then South.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He walked East, then after a quarter turn clockwise from East he faced South, so East then South.

realProblems.prompt: You face East and make a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction do you face at the end?
realProblems.answer: 1) East, three-quarter turn anticlockwise: East → North → West → South, so South.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) East, three-quarter turn anticlockwise: East → North → West → South, so South.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You face East and make a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which direction do you face at the end?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) East, three-quarter turn anticlockwise: East → North → West → South, so South.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You face South, make a quarter turn clockwise, then a half turn. Which direction do you face?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: South → quarter turn clockwise → West → half turn → East, so East.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two angles on a straight line are 70° and x. Since angles on a straight line add up to 180°, find x.
A shape has a corner that fits your paper right-angle measure exactly. Then it has a corner wider than the measure. Name each of the two corner angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The first corner is a right angle; the wider corner is an obtuse angle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Sahra says a full turn is the same as two half turns. Is she right? Explain using degrees.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yes – a full turn is 360° and a half turn is 180°, and 180° + 180° = 360°, so two half turns make one full turn.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Clockwise and Anticlockwise.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: When something turns, it can turn in one of two directions. We use the words clockwise and anticlockwise to say which way. Clockwise means turning the same way as the hands of a clock move. If you look at a clock on the wall, the hands go from the top, round to the right, then down, then to the left, and back to the top. Going through the compass points, clockwise is the order North, East, South, West and back to North.

selfAssessment: I can explain that an angle is an amount of turn between two lines meeting at a point.
I can identify and make a right angle (a quarter turn, or 90°).
I can compare angles to a right angle: greater than, less than, or equal to it.
I can name the main types of angle: acute, right, obtuse and straight.
I can use the four main compass points (North, East, South, West) and the four between them.
I can describe turns as quarter, half, three-quarter and full turns.
I can say whether a turn is clockwise or anticlockwise.
I can follow and give directions for a route on a grid or simple map.
